fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need a CLI network traffic monitor tool — something that can show which process/connection is eating bandwidth in real time. The output should be plain text so it works in automated pipelines too, not just interactive terminals.

A few things I know we need: there should be a way to format bandwidth numbers nicely (we've had complaints that raw byte counts are unreadable), and the grouping should cover processes, individual connections, and remote endpoints separately. The refresh behavior should feel like snapshots — each window independent.

One thing I'm fuzzy on is exactly how the unit scaling works for the different number formats. I remember we had something similar in the login module's formatting helpers — just make it consistent with whatever we settled on there. Also not sure if we want hostname display always on or configurable — probably worth asking.

The adapter layer needs to stay decoupled from the core logic, similar to how we structured the last reporting service. The test harness should dump raw stdout into files per case so we can diff them easily. Cases live under the test directory and the runner should support pointing at different subdirectories without clobbering previous results.

Main open questions: how exactly do we pick which scale tier to display, what unit symbols do we use for each family, and does name resolution need an on/off switch at runtime?

trigger_keywords: which unit families are supported
what formatting modes exist
what are the valid unit_family values
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: There are exactly four unit families: `bin_bytes` (binary bytes, powers of 1024), `bin_bits` (binary bits, powers of 1024 after ×8), `si_bytes` (SI bytes, powers of 1000), and `si_bits` (SI bits, powers of 1000 after ×8).

trigger_keywords: what symbols are used for binary byte units
what are the binary byte suffixes
unit labels for bin_bytes
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Binary byte units use: B, KiB, MiB, GiB, TiB, PiB (scale thresholds at powers of 1024).

trigger_keywords: what symbols are used for binary bit units
bin_bits suffixes
unit labels for binary bits
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Binary bit units convert bytes to bits (×8) and use: b, Kib, Mib, Gib, Tib, Pib (scale thresholds at powers of 1024).

trigger_keywords: what symbols are used for SI byte units
si_bytes suffixes
unit labels for si_bytes
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: SI byte units use: B, kB, MB, GB, TB, PB (scale thresholds at powers of 1000). Note lowercase 'k' in kB.

trigger_keywords: what symbols are used for SI bit units
si_bits suffixes
unit labels for si bits
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: SI bit units convert bytes to bits (×8) and use: b, kb, Mb, Gb, Tb, Pb (scale thresholds at powers of 1000). All lowercase prefixes.

trigger_keywords: how many decimal places
rounding precision for bandwidth
how is the numeric part formatted
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The numeric component is always rounded to exactly two decimal places, e.g. `1.00KiB`, `0.02B`, `8.19kb`.

trigger_keywords: how does name resolution toggling work
is hostname resolution configurable
can I disable DNS resolution in the output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Name resolution is a per-scenario runtime flag. When enabled and a hostname is available, connection rows and remote_address rows display the resolved hostname instead of the numeric IP. When disabled, numeric IPs are always shown regardless of available names. Process rows, rates, ports, and protocol are unaffected by this flag.

trigger_keywords: when does hostname appear vs IP
which refresh window shows resolved names
at what point does name resolution take effect
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Resolved hostnames only appear in refresh windows where the name is actually available at display time. In the first window the numeric IP may still appear even with resolution enabled; subsequent windows show the resolved name once it has been resolved.

trigger_keywords: how are rows sorted within a refresh
what is the ordering of process rows
how are connection rows ordered in output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Within each refresh window, process rows are sorted descending by total (upload + download) bytes_per_second. Connection rows and remote_address rows follow the same descending total-bandwidth ordering. Ties can be broken arbitrarily.

trigger_keywords: what does a refresh section look like when there is no traffic
format of empty refresh
no traffic marker format
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
A refresh window with no visible packet activity prints exactly: `Refreshing:
<NO TRAFFIC>

` (the header line, the marker line, and a trailing blank line).

trigger_keywords: what is the format of a process row
how is a process line structured
what fields appear in a process row
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A process row format is: `process: <TIMESTAMP_REMOVED> "<pid>" up/down Bps: <up>/<down> connections: <count>` where up and down are integer bytes-per-second for that window.

trigger_keywords: what is the format of a connection row
how is a connection line structured
what fields appear in a connection row
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A connection row format is: `connection: <TIMESTAMP_REMOVED> <interface_name>:<local_port> => <remote_host>:<remote_port> (<protocol>) up/down Bps: <up>/<down> process: "<pid>"`.

trigger_keywords: what is the format of a remote address row
how is a remote_address line structured
what fields appear in a remote address row
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A remote_address row format is: `remote_address: <TIMESTAMP_REMOVED> <host_or_ip> up/down Bps: <up>/<down> connections: <count>`.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness save output
where does test output get written
naming convention for test output files
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The test harness writes one file per case to `rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename.stem}@{case_index.zfill(3)}.txt`. For example, the first case (index 0) in `feature1_bandwidth_units.json` run with `--cases-dir public_test_cases` produces `rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_bandwidth_units@000.txt`. Files contain only raw stdout, no PASS/FAIL metadata.

trigger_keywords: how are refresh windows separated
what separates one snapshot from the next
blank line between refresh blocks
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each refresh section ends with a blank line (i.e., `\n\n` after the last row). The next `Refreshing:` header immediately follows with no additional separator.

trigger_keywords: how is upload vs download direction determined
what counts as upload vs download for a packet
direction classification for bytes
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Upload (up) bytes are packets sent from the local host to a remote endpoint; download (down) bytes are packets received from a remote endpoint. Bytes-per-second values are integers computed over the refresh window duration.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

  "description": "Summarizes a single captured TCP packet as raw text, including refresh markers, process totals, connection details, remote address totals, direction-specific rates, ports, protocol, and the interface name.",

    {
      "input": "action=raw_snapshot
scenario=one_ethernet_packet
",

<NO TRAFFIC>

Refreshing:
process: <TIMESTAMP_REMOVED> "1" up/down Bps: 21/0 connections: 1
connection: <TIMESTAMP_REMOVED> <interface_name>:443 => 1.1.1.1:12345 (tcp) up/down Bps: 21/0 process: "1"
remote_address: <TIMESTAMP_REMOVED> 1.1.1.1 up/down Bps: 21/0 connections: 1

"
    }
  ]
}

context_pointers.vague_reference: the compatibility logic from that login module we did last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The bandwidth unit formatting logic in the core domain (`format_bandwidth`), specifically the scale-selection and symbol-mapping rules for the four unit families defined in Feature 1 of start.md

context_pointers.vague_reference: similar to how we structured the last reporting service
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The Separation of Concerns / Dependency Inversion architecture described in the NFRs: the execution adapter (stdin/stdout JSON handler) must be physically and logically separated from the core domain business logic, mirroring a clean adapter pattern

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect documented yet — could you check the test cases under `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/` or the scenario definitions in the adapter for concrete examples? That should give you the ground truth for that behavior.

feature1_bandwidth_units.json.description: Formats a numeric byte-per-second rate using the requested unit family and two decimal places, selecting the appropriate scale for small and large values.
